rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE ALBERT SUM AND ROSE SUM REVOCABLE TRUST, DATED TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion In Support Of Motion To Enforce Subpoena Duces Tecum V City And County Of San Francisco Human Services Agency, Adult Protective Services Division
rulings.Rulings: Respondents' motion to compel is denied. Welfare and Institutions Code section 15633 provides that reports of elder abuse are confidential and may not be disclosed, except to persons to whom disclosure is permitted under Welfare and Institutions Code section 15633.5 or members of multidisciplinary teams concerned with the prevention, identification, or treatment of elder abuse. These confidentiality protections are designed to preserve the privacy interests of both alleged abusers and alleged victims of abuse, and to encourage candid reporting of suspected abuse or neglect. (See Chronicle Pub. Co. v. Sup. Ct. (1960) 54 Cal.2d 548, 569-570 and Easton v. Sutter Coast Hosp. (2000) 80 Cal.App.4th 485, 491.) Respondents contend that any "report from APS is a crucial piece of evidence to show that Petitioner abused his elderly mother." (Respondents' MPA ISO Motion to Enforce Subpoena, p.4.) They argue that they will be "significantly disadvantaged" without release of APS files. (Ibid.) Finally, they contend that "release of the requested documents" is "proper under Welfare & Institutions Code sections 15633(b)(1) and 15633.5(c)(2)." (Id., p.3.) Respondents arguments are not persuasive. First, the fact that respondents believe that it would be helpful to their case to receive confidential APS files is not a reason to override the clear confidentiality provisions established by the Legislature by enacting sections 15633 and 15633.5. The statutes explicitly require confidentiality and clearly restrict access to APS files to the entities listed in section 15633.5. Respondents, as private litigants in a trust matter, do not qualify to receive confidential APS files under any of the provisions of section 15633.5. The Court is not prepared to expand the list of individuals and entities entitled to access to the confidential files. (Adoption of Kelsey S. (1992) 1 Cal.4th 816, 826-827 [the primary role of the court " 'in construing a statute is to determine the Legislature's intent' "; "[i]f the Legislature has provided an express definition, we must take it as we find it" and may not "violate the cardinal rule that courts may not add provisions to a statute"].) Moreover, Respondents reliance on sections 15633(b)(1) and 15633.5(c)(2) is misplaced. The most those sections authorize, when read together, would be disclosure of the "identity of a person who reports" abuse to the agency in cases where "a person reporting waives confidentiality," not the broad documents discovery sought here. (See Welf. & Inst. Code sections 15633(b)(1) and 15633.5(c)(2). These sections provide no basis for the relief sought. Reliance on Welfare and Institutions Code section 10850(a) is equally misplaced. That section applies by its terms to "any provision of this code relating to any form of public social services," not APS reports. Moreover, the reference to "civil proceeding" plainly does not apply here, as this is not a "civil proceeding conducted in connection with the administration of [a public social services] program" but rather is a private dispute over a trust. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on December 2, 2020. Remote appearance is strongly encouraged, in conformity with the current General Order of the Probate and Mental Health Division, available on the court's website at https://sfsuperiorcourt.org/divisions/probate
